BGC Argo data workshop (example)¶

Workshop overview¶

  • Loading libraries
  • Fetching and loading Data
  • Filtering QC
  • Plotting data transect
  • Plotting profiles
  • Getting data from multiple floats in a giver region

    • Loading Libraries¶

    In [1]:
    # Loading libraries
import pandas as pd #A library for tabular data manipulation and analysis
import argopy as ar #A library for accessing and working with Argo float data, used here to download and process the synthetic profile data.
import numpy as np #A library for numerical operations, used here for array manipulations and calculations.
import xarray as xr #A library for working with labeled multi-dimensional arrays, used here to handle the Argo profile data in a structured format.
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt #A library for creating static, animated, and interactive visualizations in Python, used here to plot the Argo profile data.
from matplotlib.colors import LogNorm #A class for normalizing data on a logarithmic scale, used here to enhance the visualization of the Argo profile data when plotting.
from matplotlib.ticker import LogLocator #A class for locating ticks on a logarithmic scale, used here to set the tick locations when plotting the Argo profile data on a logarithmic scale.

    Xarray data structures¶

    Xarray has two fundamental data structures:

  • a DataArray, which holds a single multi-dimensional variable and its coordinates
  • a Dataset, which holds multiple variables that potentially share the same coordinates

    DataArray
    A DataArray has four essential attributes:

    values: a numpy.ndarray holding the array’s values dims: dimension names for each axis (e.g., ('x', 'y', 'z')) coords: a dict-like container of arrays (coordinates) that label each point (e.g., 1-dimensional arrays of numbers, datetime objects or strings) attrs: an OrderedDict to hold arbitrary metadata (attributes)

    • Fetching data¶

    The BGC-Argo data files are store on the Global Data Assembly Centre (GDAC) following a specific data storage architecture.

    Each Data assembly centre has its own repertory that contains as many folder as there are floats. In each float folder you will find different NetCDF data files

    • Rtraj Provide information on the position of the float, its cycling pattern and other useful metadata
    • meta Contains all the metadata of the float
    • Sprof This is the most useful file for most scientific usage. It's a concatenation of all the profiles with all the observed variables.
    • tech Is a summary of the technical, engineering specs of the floats, like battery consumption or autonomy.
    • profils/ This repertory has all the profiles in separate NetCDF, it can be useful for specific needs, but we won't cover this in this workshop

    But how do we find a specific file ?¶

    When we want to download data from the float array, we want to be able to query all the float in specific location or with a specific payload. And even when we know which float data we want to download we don't necesseraly know in which DAC folder it is stored.

    To find your way in the data archive you need to download the Bio-Index file, which is a table listing all the float profile with their longitude, latitude, date, payload and the path of the data file in the GDAC.

    We can download it with this simple utility function

    In [2]:
    import os #A library for interacting with the operating system, used here to handle file paths and directories.
import requests #A library for making HTTP requests, used here to download the synthetic profile index file from the specified URL.

#Provide the URL where the synthetic profile index file is located, the URL for the DAC, the local directory to save the data, and the filename to save as. Then, download the file and save it to the specified location.
host = 'https://data-argo.ifremer.fr/argo_synthetic-profile_index.txt'
dac = 'https://data-argo.ifremer.fr/dac'
data_directory = 'Data'
filename = 'synth_file.txt'
destination = os.path.join(data_directory, filename)

#setting data directory of DSP
if not os.path.exists(data_directory):
    os.makedirs(data_directory)

response = requests.get(host, stream=True)

with open(destination, "wb") as f:
    r = requests.get(host)
    f.write(r.content)
    print(f"File downloaded and saved to {destination}")

    File downloaded and saved to Data\synth_file.txt

    You can now have a look at the file.

    I'm using the read_csv function from pandas as the synth file is structured in a tabular format.

    In [3]:
    index_file = pd.read_csv('../Data/synth_file.txt', sep=',', skiprows=8)

#Use the head() method to display the first few rows of the index file, which contains metadata about the synthetic profiles available for download.
index_file.head()
    Out[3]:
    file date latitude longitude ocean profiler_type institution parameters parameter_data_mode date_update
    0 aoml/1900722/profiles/SD1900722_001.nc 2.006102e+13 -40.316 73.389 I 846 AO PRES TEMP PSAL DOXY DDDD 20220628080801
    1 aoml/1900722/profiles/SD1900722_002.nc 2.006110e+13 -40.390 73.528 I 846 AO PRES TEMP PSAL DOXY DDDD 20220628080813
    2 aoml/1900722/profiles/SD1900722_003.nc 2.006111e+13 -40.455 73.335 I 846 AO PRES TEMP PSAL DOXY DDDD 20220628080826
    3 aoml/1900722/profiles/SD1900722_004.nc 2.006112e+13 -40.134 73.080 I 846 AO PRES TEMP PSAL DOXY DDDD 20220628080839
    4 aoml/1900722/profiles/SD1900722_005.nc 2.006120e+13 -39.641 73.158 I 846 AO PRES TEMP PSAL DOXY DDDD 20220628080852

    So, technically, we can now make any request on that table t o find the float profiles that would be interesting to us and use the "file" columns to find the ftp adress to download the file.

    It sounds a bit cumbersome, doesn't it ?

    happilty for us, a packahe has recently been developped that will make our life easier.

    Argopy¶

    We will use the Argopy library to fetch and load data from a BGC Argo float. The documentation is available at Argopy documentation.

    We can directly load the Sprof file of any BGC-Argo float in memory, simply by calling a ar.ArgoFloat class which will fetch the data from the GDAC.

    The WMO argument will allow us to specify which float we want to use.

    In [4]:
    # example of sensor issue with bad Qc result WMO = 5907147
WMO = 3901586
#The ar.ARgofloat class is used to create an instance of an Argo float with the specified WMO number.
# The open_dataset method is then called with the argument "Sprof" to access the synthetic profile data for that float, which is stored in the variable argo_ds as an xarray Dataset.
argo_ds = ar.ArgoFloat(WMO).open_dataset("Sprof")

    So, now the argo_ds is the xarray object containing the Sprof data of the WMo we provided. We can easily visualise the file structure using the xr.display function.

    In [5]:
    display(argo_ds)

    <xarray.Dataset> Size: 101MB
Dimensions:                            (N_PROF: 122, N_PARAM: 14, N_CALIB: 1,
                                        N_LEVELS: 2334)
Dimensions without coordinates: N_PROF, N_PARAM, N_CALIB, N_LEVELS
Data variables: (12/128)
    DATA_TYPE                          <U32 128B ...
    FORMAT_VERSION                     <U4 16B ...
    HANDBOOK_VERSION                   <U4 16B ...
    REFERENCE_DATE_TIME                datetime64[ns] 8B ...
    DATE_CREATION                      datetime64[ns] 8B ...
    DATE_UPDATE                        datetime64[ns] 8B ...
    ...                                 ...
    NITRATE                            (N_PROF, N_LEVELS) float32 1MB ...
    NITRATE_QC                         (N_PROF, N_LEVELS) int32 1MB ...
    NITRATE_dPRES                      (N_PROF, N_LEVELS) float32 1MB ...
    NITRATE_ADJUSTED                   (N_PROF, N_LEVELS) float32 1MB ...
    NITRATE_ADJUSTED_QC                (N_PROF, N_LEVELS) int32 1MB ...
    NITRATE_ADJUSTED_ERROR             (N_PROF, N_LEVELS) float32 1MB ...
Attributes:
    title:                Argo float vertical profile
    institution:          BODC
    source:               Argo float
    history:              2026-03-07T16:07:13Z creation (software version 1.2...
    references:           http://www.argodatamgt.org/Documentation
    user_manual_version:  1.0
    Conventions:          Argo-3.1 CF-1.6
    featureType:          trajectoryProfile
    software_version:     1.20 (version 12.05.2025 for ARGO_simplified_profile)
    id:                   https://doi.org/10.17882/42182
    xarray.Dataset
      • N_PROF: 122
      • N_PARAM: 14
      • N_CALIB: 1
      • N_LEVELS: 2334
        • DATA_TYPE
          ()
          <U32
          ...
          long_name :
          Data type
          conventions :
          Argo reference table 1
          casted :
          1
          [1 values with dtype=<U32]
        • FORMAT_VERSION
          ()
          <U4
          ...
          long_name :
          File format version
          casted :
          1
          [1 values with dtype=<U4]
        • HANDBOOK_VERSION
          ()
          <U4
          ...
          long_name :
          Data handbook version
          casted :
          1
          [1 values with dtype=<U4]
        • REFERENCE_DATE_TIME
          ()
          datetime64[ns]
          ...
          long_name :
          Date of reference for Julian days
          conventions :
          YYYYMMDDHHMISS
          casted :
          1
          [1 values with dtype=datetime64[ns]]
        • DATE_CREATION
          ()
          datetime64[ns]
          ...
          long_name :
          Date of file creation
          conventions :
          YYYYMMDDHHMISS
          casted :
          1
          [1 values with dtype=datetime64[ns]]
        • DATE_UPDATE
          ()
          datetime64[ns]
          ...
          long_name :
          Date of update of this file
          conventions :
          YYYYMMDDHHMISS
          casted :
          1
          [1 values with dtype=datetime64[ns]]
        • PLATFORM_NUMBER
          (N_PROF)
          int32
          ...
          long_name :
          Float unique identifier
          conventions :
          WMO float identifier : A9IIIII
          casted :
          1
          [122 values with dtype=int32]
        • PROJECT_NAME
          (N_PROF)
          <U64
          ...
          long_name :
          Name of the project
          casted :
          1
          [122 values with dtype=<U64]
        • PI_NAME
          (N_PROF)
          <U64
          ...
          long_name :
          Name of the principal investigator
          casted :
          1
          [122 values with dtype=<U64]
        • STATION_PARAMETERS
          (N_PROF, N_PARAM)
          <U64
          ...
          long_name :
          List of available parameters for the station
          conventions :
          Argo reference table 3
          casted :
          1
          [1708 values with dtype=<U64]
        • CYCLE_NUMBER
          (N_PROF)
          int32
          ...
          long_name :
          Float cycle number
          conventions :
          0...N, 0 : launch cycle (if exists), 1 : first complete cycle
          casted :
          1
          [122 values with dtype=int32]
        • DIRECTION
          (N_PROF)
          <U1
          ...
          long_name :
          Direction of the station profiles
          conventions :
          A: ascending profiles, D: descending profiles
          casted :
          1
          [122 values with dtype=<U1]
        • DATA_CENTRE
          (N_PROF)
          <U2
          ...
          long_name :
          Data centre in charge of float data processing
          conventions :
          Argo reference table 4
          casted :
          1
          [122 values with dtype=<U2]
        • PARAMETER_DATA_MODE
          (N_PROF, N_PARAM)
          <U1
          ...
          long_name :
          Delayed mode or real time data
          conventions :
          R : real time; D : delayed mode; A : real time with adjustment
          casted :
          1
          [1708 values with dtype=<U1]
        • PLATFORM_TYPE
          (N_PROF)
          <U32
          ...
          long_name :
          Type of float
          conventions :
          Argo reference table 23
          casted :
          1
          [122 values with dtype=<U32]
        • FLOAT_SERIAL_NO
          (N_PROF)
          <U32
          ...
          long_name :
          Serial number of the float
          casted :
          1
          [122 values with dtype=<U32]
        • FIRMWARE_VERSION
          (N_PROF)
          <U32
          ...

          casted :
          1
          [122 values with dtype=<U32]

          (N_PROF)
          int32
          ...
          long_name :

          Argo reference table 8

          1
          [122 values with dtype=int32]

          (N_PROF)
          datetime64[ns]
          ...
          long_name :
          Julian day (UTC) of the station relative to REFERENCE_DATE_TIME

          time
          conventions :
          Relative julian days with decimal part (as parts of day)
          axis :
          T
          resolution :
          0.0006944444444444445
          casted :
          1
          [122 values with dtype=datetime64[ns]]

          [122 values with dtype=int32]

        • title :
          Argo float vertical profile
          institution :
          BODC
          source :
          Argo float
          history :
          2026-03-07T16:07:13Z creation (software version 1.20 (version 12.05.2025 for ARGO_simplified_profile))
          references :
          http://www.argodatamgt.org/Documentation
          user_manual_version :
          1.0
          Conventions :
          Argo-3.1 CF-1.6
          featureType :
          trajectoryProfile
          software_version :
          1.20 (version 12.05.2025 for ARGO_simplified_profile)
          id :
          https://doi.org/10.17882/42182

        Great, we have found the full BGC data for this float.

        We can see that the profile number N_PROF and the depth level N_LEVELS are two of the dimensions. This will be useful later on to visualise and extract data.

        Checking QC¶

        The argo is using a standardised data quality control framework. Each QC test can be found in the different variables cookbook and the result is stored in a controled QC vocabulary scheme.

        In [6]:
        argo_ds = argo_ds.rename({'CYCLE_NUMBER':'PROF_NUM'}).swap_dims({'N_PROF':'PROF_NUM'})
display(argo_ds)

        <xarray.Dataset> Size: 101MB

        • title :
          Argo float vertical profile
          institution :
          BODC
          source :
          Argo float
          history :
          2026-03-07T16:07:13Z creation (software version 1.20 (version 12.05.2025 for ARGO_simplified_profile))
          references :
          http://www.argodatamgt.org/Documentation
          user_manual_version :
          1.0
          Conventions :
          Argo-3.1 CF-1.6
          featureType :
          trajectoryProfile
          software_version :
          1.20 (version 12.05.2025 for ARGO_simplified_profile)
          id :
          https://doi.org/10.17882/42182
        In [7]:
        # Stack NumPy array of datetimes to create a 2D grid
time_2D = np.tile(argo_ds['JULD'].values,(len(argo_ds['N_LEVELS']),1)).T

# Function for repetitive parts of plot
def config_depth_section(cbar_label,title):
  plt.ylim([0,250])
  plt.gca().invert_yaxis()
  plt.ylabel('Pressure (dbar)')
  if 'Nitrate' in cbar_label: extend = 'min'
  else:                       extend = 'neither'
  plt.colorbar(label=cbar_label,extend=extend)
  plt.title(title)

# Create subplots
plt.figure(figsize=(16,6))
plt.subplot(2,2,1)
cmap = plt.get_cmap('viridis')
cmap.set_under('0.5')
plt.pcolor(time_2D,argo_ds['PRES'].values,argo_ds['CHLA'].values,cmap=cmap)
config_depth_section('Nitrate (µmol/kg)',"Parameter: 'CHLA'")

plt.subplot(2,2,2)
plt.pcolor(time_2D,argo_ds['PRES'].values,argo_ds['CHLA_QC'].values.astype(float),cmap='Set1',vmin=0.5,vmax=9.5)
config_depth_section('QC flag',"Parameter: 'CHLA_QC'")

plt.subplot(2,2,3)
plt.pcolor(time_2D,argo_ds['PRES'].values,argo_ds['PSAL'].values,cmap=cmap)
config_depth_section('Nitrate (µmol/kg)',"Parameter: 'PSAL'")

plt.subplot(2,2,4)
plt.pcolor(time_2D,argo_ds['PRES'].values,argo_ds['PSAL_QC'].values.astype(float),cmap='Set1',vmin=0.5,vmax=9.5)
config_depth_section('QC flag',"Parameter: 'PSAL_QC'")

plt.tight_layout(h_pad=5,w_pad=3)

        C:\Users\petit\AppData\Local\Temp\ipykernel_70964\414752045.py:19: UserWarning: The input coordinates to pcolor are interpreted as cell centers, but are not monotonically increasing or decreasing. This may lead to incorrectly calculated cell edges, in which case, please supply explicit cell edges to pcolor.
  plt.pcolor(time_2D,argo_ds['PRES'].values,argo_ds['CHLA'].values,cmap=cmap)

This may lead to incorrectly calculated cell edges, in which case, please supply explicit cell edges to pcolor.

This may lead to incorrectly calculated cell edges, in which case, please supply explicit cell edges to pcolor.
  plt.pcolor(time_2D,argo_ds['PRES'].values,argo_ds['PSAL'].values,cmap=cmap)
C:\Users\petit\AppData\Local\Temp\ipykernel_70964\414752045.py:31: UserWarning: The input coordinates to pcolor are interpreted as cell centers, but are not monotonically increasing or decreasing. This may lead to incorrectly calculated cell edges, in which case, please supply explicit cell edges to pcolor.
  plt.pcolor(time_2D,argo_ds['PRES'].values,argo_ds['PSAL_QC'].values.astype(float),cmap='Set1',vmin=0.5,vmax=9.5)

        Basic plotting¶

        Mapping¶

        We can first easily visualise the trajectory of the float with a scatter plot.

        In [8]:
        import cartopy.crs as ccrs
import cartopy
import pandas as pd
from typing import cast
from cartopy.mpl.geoaxes import GeoAxes
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.dates as mdates


def plot_argo_trajectory(argo_ds, extent=10):
  import cartopy.feature as cfeature

  dates = pd.to_datetime(argo_ds["JULD"].values)
  date_nums = mdates.date2num(dates)

  lon = argo_ds["LONGITUDE"].values.astype(float)
  lat = argo_ds["LATITUDE"].values.astype(float)
  valid = (~pd.isna(lon)) & (~pd.isna(lat))

  lon = lon[valid]
  lat = lat[valid]
  date_nums = date_nums[valid]

  fig, ax0 = plt.subplots(
    figsize=(10, 10),
    subplot_kw={"projection": ccrs.PlateCarree()},
  )
  ax = cast(GeoAxes, ax0)

  pad = float(extent) if extent is not None else 2.0
  ax.set_extent(
    [lon.min() - pad, lon.max() + pad, lat.min() - pad, lat.max() + pad],
    crs=ccrs.PlateCarree(),
  )

  # Bathymetry-like shaded relief background
  ax.add_feature(cfeature.OCEAN.with_scale("10m"), facecolor="#c6dbef", zorder=0)
  ax.add_feature(cfeature.LAND.with_scale("10m"), facecolor="0.9", zorder=2)
  ax.add_feature(cfeature.COASTLINE.with_scale("10m"), edgecolor="black", linewidth=0.7, zorder=3)

  sc = ax.scatter(
    lon,
    lat,
    c=date_nums,
    cmap="viridis",
    s=25,
    transform=ccrs.PlateCarree(),
    label="Profiles",
    zorder=4,
  )

  cbar = fig.colorbar(sc, ax=ax, orientation="vertical", pad=0.02)
  cbar.set_label("Date of the profile (JULD)")
  cbar.ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter("%b %Y"))
  cbar.update_ticks()

  gl = ax.gridlines(draw_labels=True, dms=True, x_inline=False, y_inline=False)
  gl.top_labels = False
  gl.right_labels = False

  ax.legend(loc="lower left")
  ax.set_title(f"Float {argo_ds.PLATFORM_NUMBER[0].astype(str).values} trajectory")
  ax.set_xlabel("Longitude")
  ax.set_ylabel("Latitude")
  plt.show()
        In [9]:
        plot_argo_trajectory(argo_ds, extent=10)

        Cycle period¶

        An other very important sampling parameter is the time elapsed between two profiles. The standard mission configuration is about 10 days, but you can check the actual cycle period by looking at the time difference between two profiles.

        In [10]:
        # Build a clean profile-time series
times = pd.to_datetime(argo_ds["JULD"].values)
times = pd.Series(times).dropna().sort_values().reset_index(drop=True)

# Time difference between consecutive profiles (days)
dt_days = times.diff().dt.total_seconds() / 86400.0

# Plot: x = actual profile time, y = time difference to previous profile
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 4))
ax.plot(times.iloc[1:], dt_days.iloc[1:], "-o", ms=4)
ax.set_xlabel("Profile time")
ax.set_ylabel("Time difference to previous profile (days)")
ax.set_title(f"Float {WMO} - Time gap between consecutive profiles")
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
        In [11]:
        profile_time_table = (
    pd.DataFrame(
        {
            "PROF_NUM": argo_ds["PROF_NUM"].values,
            "profile_datetime": pd.to_datetime(argo_ds["JULD"].values),
        }
    )
    .dropna(subset=["profile_datetime"])
    .sort_values("profile_datetime")
    .reset_index(drop=True)
)

profile_time_table["elapsed_since_last_profile"] = profile_time_table["profile_datetime"].diff()
profile_time_table["elapsed_since_last_profile_days"] = (
    profile_time_table["elapsed_since_last_profile"].dt.total_seconds() / 86400
).round(3)

profile_time_table
        Out[11]:
        PROF_NUM profile_datetime elapsed_since_last_profile elapsed_since_last_profile_days
        0 1 2024-10-01 06:31:25.000792576 NaT NaN
        1 1 2024-10-11 13:50:24.998305280 10 days 07:18:59.997512704 10.305
        2 2 2024-10-11 14:26:59.000759808 0 days 00:36:34.002454528 0.025
        3 2 2024-10-22 01:26:44.998124544 10 days 10:59:45.997364736 10.458
        4 3 2024-10-24 13:42:24.992623616 2 days 12:15:39.994499072 2.511
        ... ... ... ... ...
        117 116 2026-01-26 13:28:45.994410496 10 days 00:01:59.001691136 10.001
        118 117 2026-02-05 12:03:07.996258304 9 days 22:34:22.001847808 9.941
        119 118 2026-02-15 13:36:46.992773632 10 days 01:33:38.996515328 10.065
        120 119 2026-02-25 13:30:46.994421248 9 days 23:54:00.001647616 9.996
        121 120 2026-03-07 09:14:01.996387328 9 days 19:43:15.001966080 9.822

        122 rows × 4 columns

        In [12]:
        def format_for_transect_plotting(argo_ds, max_depth=1000):
    """
    Prepare Argo profile data for transect plotting on a common pressure grid.
    Returns:
        juld, prei, pres, psal, temp, psali, tempi, chlai
    """
    prei = np.arange(5, max_depth, 5)

    juld = argo_ds["JULD"].values
    pres = argo_ds["PRES"].values
    psal = argo_ds["PSAL"].values if "PSAL" in argo_ds else None
    temp = argo_ds["TEMP"].values if "TEMP" in argo_ds else None
    chla = argo_ds["CHLA"].values if "CHLA" in argo_ds else None

    n_prof = pres.shape[0]
    n_prei = prei.shape[0]

    psali = np.full((n_prof, n_prei), np.nan, dtype=float)
    tempi = np.full((n_prof, n_prei), np.nan, dtype=float)
    chlai = np.full((n_prof, n_prei), np.nan, dtype=float)  # placeholder if CHLA not in `prof`

    for ip in range(n_prof):
        p = pres[ip, :]
        m_p = np.isfinite(p)

        if psal is not None:
            v = psal[ip, :]
            m = m_p & np.isfinite(v)
            if m.sum() > 1:
                pp, vv = p[m], v[m]
                order = np.argsort(pp)
                pp, vv = pp[order], vv[order]
                pp_u, idx = np.unique(pp, return_index=True)
                psali[ip, :] = np.interp(prei, pp_u, vv[idx], left=np.nan, right=np.nan)

        if temp is not None:
            v = temp[ip, :]
            m = m_p & np.isfinite(v)
            if m.sum() > 1:
                pp, vv = p[m], v[m]
                order = np.argsort(pp)
                pp, vv = pp[order], vv[order]
                pp_u, idx = np.unique(pp, return_index=True)
                tempi[ip, :] = np.interp(prei, pp_u, vv[idx], left=np.nan, right=np.nan)
        if chla is not None:
            v = chla[ip, :]
            m = m_p & np.isfinite(v)
            if m.sum() > 1:
                pp, vv = p[m], v[m]
                order = np.argsort(pp)
                pp, vv = pp[order], vv[order]
                pp_u, idx = np.unique(pp, return_index=True)
                chlai[ip, :] = np.interp(prei, pp_u, vv[idx], left=np.nan, right=np.nan)

    return juld, prei, pres, psal, temp, psali, tempi, chlai


# usage (keeps your existing variable names)
juld, prei, pres, psal, temp, psali, tempi, chlai = format_for_transect_plotting(argo_ds, max_depth=1000)
        In [13]:
        fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(15,10))

#Draw the contours for the salinity
cs=ax.contourf(juld,prei,tempi.transpose(),40,cmap="RdBu_r")

#Draw the contours lines to be labelled
cs2=ax.contour(juld,prei,tempi.transpose(),colors=('k'), levels=cs.levels[::8])

#Since pressure increase away from the surface we invert the y-axis
ax.invert_yaxis()
ax.clabel(cs2, fmt='%2.1f', colors='w', fontsize=10)

#Add the titles
ax.set_title(f"Vertical Temperature section for float {argo_ds.PLATFORM_NUMBER[0].astype(str).values}")
ax.set_xlabel(f"{argo_ds.JULD.standard_name}")
ax.set_ylabel(f"{argo_ds.PRES.long_name}")

#Add the colorbar
cbar=fig.colorbar(cs,ax=ax)

        We can visualise the transect of the float with a bit more data manipulation. We will regrid the data in depth and time, and then plot the data as a 2D image.

        In [14]:
        fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(15,10))

#Draw the contours for the salinity
cs=ax.contourf(juld,prei,chlai.transpose(),40,cmap="Greens")

#Draw the contours lines to be labelled
cs2=ax.contour(juld,prei,chlai.transpose(),colors=('k'), levels=cs.levels[::10])

#Since pressure increase away from the surface we invert the y-axis
ax.invert_yaxis()
ax.clabel(cs2, fmt='%2.1f', colors='w', fontsize=10)

#Add the titles
ax.set_title(f"Vertical Chlorophyll section for float {argo_ds.PLATFORM_NUMBER[0].astype(str).values}")
ax.set_xlabel(f"{argo_ds.JULD.standard_name}")
ax.set_ylabel(f"{argo_ds.PRES.long_name}")
ax.set_ylim([250,0])

#Add the colorbar
cbar=fig.colorbar(cs,ax=ax, label='Chlorophyll-a (mg/m^3)')
        In [15]:
        def plot_transect(
    ds,
    varname,
    depth_max=200,
    log_transform=False,
    palette="viridis",
    vmin=None,
    vmax=None,
):
    """
    Plot a depth-vs-time transect from profile-form datasets.

    Parameters
    ----------
    ds : xarray.Dataset
        Input dataset (e.g., argo_ds)
    varname : str
        Variable to plot (e.g., 'CHLA', 'TEMP', 'NITRATE')
    depth_max : int or float
        Maximum depth (dbar) for interpolation/plotting
    log_transform : bool
        If True, apply log10 transform to positive values before plotting
    palette : str
        Matplotlib colormap name
    vmin : float or None
        Minimum colorbar limit
    vmax : float or None
        Maximum colorbar limit
    """

    if varname not in ds:
        raise KeyError(f"'{varname}' not found in dataset.")
    if "PRES" not in ds:
        raise KeyError("'PRES' not found in dataset.")
    if vmin is not None and vmax is not None and vmin >= vmax:
        raise ValueError("'vmin' must be smaller than 'vmax'.")

    time_name = "TIME" if "TIME" in ds else ("JULD" if "JULD" in ds else None)
    if time_name is None:
        raise KeyError("No time variable found. Expected 'TIME' or 'JULD'.")

    var = ds[varname].values
    pres = ds["PRES"].values
    time_vals = ds[time_name].values
    depth_grid = np.arange(0, depth_max + 1, 1)

    profiles = []
    times_unique = time_vals
    n_prof = var.shape[0]

    for i in range(n_prof):
        p = pres[i, :]
        v = var[i, :]
        m = np.isfinite(p) & np.isfinite(v)
        if m.sum() > 1:
            p_valid = p[m]
            v_valid = v[m]
            order = np.argsort(p_valid)
            p_valid = p_valid[order]
            v_valid = v_valid[order]
            p_unique, idx = np.unique(p_valid, return_index=True)
            v_interp = np.interp(depth_grid, p_unique, v_valid[idx], left=np.nan, right=np.nan)
        else:
            v_interp = np.full_like(depth_grid, np.nan, dtype=float)
        profiles.append(v_interp)

    section = np.array(profiles, dtype=float)

    if log_transform:
        section = np.where(section > 0, np.log10(section), np.nan)
        cbar_label = f"log10({varname})"
    else:
        cbar_label = varname

    if vmin is not None and vmax is not None:
        extend = "both"
    elif vmin is not None:
        extend = "min"
    elif vmax is not None:
        extend = "max"
    else:
        extend = "neither"

    plt.figure(figsize=(13, 6))
    pcm = plt.pcolormesh(
        times_unique,
        depth_grid,
        section.T,
        shading="auto",
        cmap=palette,
        vmin=vmin,
        vmax=vmax,
    )
    plt.gca().invert_yaxis()
    plt.xlabel(time_name)
    plt.ylabel("Pressure (dbar)")
    plt.title(f"{varname} transect")
    plt.colorbar(pcm, label=cbar_label, extend=extend)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
        In [16]:
        plot_transect(argo_ds, "BBP700", depth_max=2000, log_transform=True)

        C:\Users\petit\AppData\Local\Temp\ipykernel_70964\2842616356.py:70: RuntimeWarning: invalid value encountered in log10
  section = np.where(section > 0, np.log10(section), np.nan)
        In [17]:
        plot_transect(argo_ds, "CHLA_ADJUSTED", depth_max=2000, log_transform=True, palette="Greens")

        C:\Users\petit\AppData\Local\Temp\ipykernel_70964\2842616356.py:70: RuntimeWarning: divide by zero encountered in log10
  section = np.where(section > 0, np.log10(section), np.nan)
C:\Users\petit\AppData\Local\Temp\ipykernel_70964\2842616356.py:70: RuntimeWarning: invalid value encountered in log10
  section = np.where(section > 0, np.log10(section), np.nan)
        In [18]:
        plot_transect(argo_ds, "DOXY", depth_max=2000, log_transform=False, palette = "RdBu_r", vmax = 280, vmin = 220)

        Profile based analysis¶

        After visualising the full float time series transect we often want to go into more details and look at discrete profiles. The PROF_NUM dimension of the SProf will be useful to do so.

        In [19]:
        # Choose any available profile number here:
profile_num = int(argo_ds["PROF_NUM"].values[10])  

# Select the profile based on the PROF_NUM dimension
prof = argo_ds.sel(PROF_NUM=profile_num)

# Extract and clean data
p = prof["PRES"].values
t = prof["TEMP"].values
m = np.isfinite(p) & np.isfinite(t)

# Plot temperature profile
plt.figure(figsize=(5, 7))
plt.plot(t[m], p[m], "-o", ms=3)
plt.gca().invert_yaxis()
plt.xlabel("Temperature (°C)")
plt.ylabel("Pressure (dbar)")
plt.title(f"Temperature profile - PROF_NUM {profile_num}")
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

        Something very handy with NetCDf is the capacity to automatically label long names and units of variable. Because it is a self describing format, all the meta-information can be found in thee attributes. Let's build a little function that return a string with the long name of a variable and its units.

        In [20]:
        def _label_from_attrs(da):
    """Extract a formatted label from the attributes of a DataArray.

    Args:
        da (_type_): The DataArray from which to extract the label.

    Returns:
        _type_: The formatted label string.
    """    
    name = da.attrs.get("long_name") or da.attrs.get("standard_name") or da.name
    units = da.attrs.get("units")
    return f"{name} ({units})" if units else name

#example making a label fo temperature using the function we just defined:
_label_from_attrs(prof["TEMP"])
        Out[20]:
        'Sea temperature in-situ ITS-90 scale (degree_Celsius)'

        So, if we do the same plot as above with a cleaner label for X and Y axis

        In [21]:
        # Plot temperature profile
plt.figure(figsize=(5, 7))
plt.plot(t[m], p[m], "-o", ms=3)
plt.gca().invert_yaxis()
plt.xlabel(_label_from_attrs(prof["TEMP"]))
plt.ylabel(_label_from_attrs(prof["PRES"]))
plt.title(f"Temperature profile - PROF_NUM {profile_num}")
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

        Now, we can go a little bit further and make a grid plot to look at different variables of a same profile, we will follow the same logic, subsetting our dataset with isel function and looping through variables, automating the labelling using our label_from_attrs function

        In [22]:
        # 2x2 profile grid: TEMP, PSAL, DOXY, CHLA using variable attributes for labels
vars_to_plot = ["TEMP", "PSAL", "DOXY", "CHLA"]

prof = argo_ds.sel(PROF_NUM=profile_num)

#define our 2*2 grid of subplots, sharing the y-axis (pressure) for easier comparison across variables
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(12, 10), sharey=True)

#extract the PRESSURE var and make a Y axis label
p = prof["PRES"].values
p_label = _label_from_attrs(prof["PRES"])


#Now we through our plots grid (ax) and our variables to plot (var) in parallel,
# checking if the variable is in the profile dataset, and if so,
# plotting it against pressure.
for ax, var in zip(axes.ravel(), vars_to_plot):
    if var in prof:
        x = prof[var].values
        m = np.isfinite(p) & np.isfinite(x)
        ax.plot(x[m], p[m], "-o", ms=2)
        ax.set_xlabel(_label_from_attrs(prof[var]))
        ax.set_title(prof[var].attrs.get("long_name", var))
        ax.invert_yaxis()
    else:
        ax.text(0.5, 0.5, f"{var} not available", ha="center", va="center", transform=ax.transAxes)
        ax.set_title(var)

    #ax.invert_yaxis()
    ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

axes[0, 0].set_ylabel(p_label)
axes[1, 0].set_ylabel(p_label)

fig.suptitle(f"Profile {profile_num}: TEMP / PSAL / DOXY / CHLA", y=1.02)
plt.gca().invert_yaxis()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

        Obviously, looking for a profile by its number is not the most handy way to perform data analysis. Most of the time we want to see profile withing a giver date range. In this case, we use the same coding loging but we perform a where filtering on the JULD dimension. When providing a date range it is likely we will end up with multiple profiles insead of just one, so the plotting will be a bit different. We will loop through the profiles to color code by date.

        In [23]:
        # Select all profiles in a date range
start_date = np.datetime64("2025-07-01")
end_date = np.datetime64("2025-12-01")
mask_time = (argo_ds["JULD"] >= start_date) & (argo_ds["JULD"] < end_date)

prof_nums_july = argo_ds["PROF_NUM"].where(mask_time, drop=True)
ds_july = argo_ds.sel(PROF_NUM=prof_nums_july)

dates = pd.to_datetime(ds_july["JULD"].values)
date_nums = mdates.date2num(dates)

cmap = plt.get_cmap("viridis")
norm = plt.Normalize(date_nums.min(), date_nums.max())

plt.figure(figsize=(6, 8))

ax = plt.gca()

# Plot each profile, colored by date
for prof_num, dnum in zip(ds_july["PROF_NUM"].values, date_nums):
    prof = ds_july.sel(PROF_NUM=prof_num)

    p = prof["PRES"].values
    t = prof["TEMP"].values
    m = np.isfinite(p) & np.isfinite(t)

    ax.plot(t[m], p[m], "-", color=cmap(norm(dnum)), lw=1.5, alpha=0.9)

ax.invert_yaxis()
ax.set_xlabel(_label_from_attrs(ds_july["TEMP"]))
ax.set_ylabel(_label_from_attrs(ds_july["PRES"]))
ax.set_title("Temperature profiles - Summer 2025")
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

# Color guide
sm = plt.cm.ScalarMappable(cmap=cmap, norm=norm)
sm.set_array([])
cbar = plt.colorbar(sm, ax=ax, pad=0.02, ticks=np.unique(date_nums))
cbar.set_label("Profile date")
cbar.ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter("%d %b %Y"))

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

        In [ ]:
        # Variables you want to plot
vars_to_plot = ["TEMP", "PSAL", "DOXY", "CHLA"]  # change as needed

# Select profiles in date range
start_date = np.datetime64("2025-07-01")
end_date   = np.datetime64("2025-12-01")

mask_time = (argo_ds["JULD"] >= start_date) & (argo_ds["JULD"] < end_date)

prof_nums = argo_ds["PROF_NUM"].where(mask_time, drop=True)
ds_sel = argo_ds.sel(PROF_NUM=prof_nums)

dates = pd.to_datetime(ds_sel["JULD"].values)
date_nums = mdates.date2num(dates)

# Shared colormap / normalization
cmap = plt.get_cmap("viridis")
norm = plt.Normalize(date_nums.min(), date_nums.max())

# Create grid
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(10, 10), sharey=True)
axes = axes.ravel()

for ax, var in zip(axes, vars_to_plot):

    for prof_num, dnum in zip(ds_sel["PROF_NUM"].values, date_nums):
        prof = ds_sel.sel(PROF_NUM=prof_num)

        p = prof["PRES"].values
        v = prof[var].values

        m = np.isfinite(p) & np.isfinite(v)

        ax.plot(v[m], p[m],
                "-", color=cmap(norm(dnum)),
                lw=1.5, alpha=0.9)
        
    ax.set_xlabel(_label_from_attrs(ds_sel[var]))
    ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
    ax.set_title(var)

# Only left plots need y-labels
axes[0].invert_yaxis()
axes[0].set_ylabel(_label_from_attrs(ds_sel["PRES"]))
axes[2].set_ylabel(_label_from_attrs(ds_sel["PRES"]))

# Shared colorbar
# Create space on the right for the colorbar
fig.subplots_adjust(right=0.88)

# Dedicated axis for colorbar  → [left, bottom, width, height]
cax = fig.add_axes([0.90, 0.15, 0.02, 0.7])

sm = plt.cm.ScalarMappable(cmap=cmap, norm=norm)
sm.set_array([])

cbar = fig.colorbar(sm, cax=cax)
cbar.set_label("Profile date")
cbar.ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(
    mdates.DateFormatter("%d %b %Y")
)
plt.show()

        Fecthing BGC-Argo data on a given location¶

        Now that we know how to look for a specific BGC-Argo float and how to extract its data, metadata and QC we can start doing analysis on entire regions based on the same coding practice !

        A few caveats come into place though :

        • Merging different BGC-Argo dataset can be tricky since the depth levels will be different
        • The cycle timing can be different
        • You may not have the same variable combination (sensor payloads) and the floats you've fetched

        Happily for us, again, Argopy tried to solved most of those issues !

        We will first need to define our fetching criteria :

        A box with longitude, latitude, date and depth boundaries.

        In [24]:
        with ar.set_options(ds='bgc'):
    f = ar.DataFetcher(params='DOXY').region([-35, -20, 55, 70,0, 100, '2024-05', '2024-10'])

        Before loading the entire dataset into memory you can already see what float you have in your fecthing box with the plot functions of argopy.

        In [25]:
        f.plot()
        Out[25]:
        (<Figure size 900x540 with 1 Axes>,
 <GeoAxes: xlabel='longitude', ylabel='latitude'>)

        Now, depending on the box you've chosen you can see that you have one or several floats in your location.

        The .load method will load the data from those floats into memory in a unified data structure.

        In [26]:
        dat = f.to_xarray()
        In [ ]:
        display(dat)

        What difference do you notice with a Sprof file ?

        • The dimensions are changed
        • The Coordinates are also changed
        • The JULD variable is now a TIME variable.

        Homework¶
